ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai spicy glass noodles with chicken and vegetables. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Noodle dishes) is the closest available category. No specific code exists for Thai glass noodle dishes with chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Thai noodle preparation.

preparation_method: seasoned with chilli and fish sauce
main_ingredients: glass noodles (tang hoon / mung bean vermicelli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy glass noodles with chicken and vegetables is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-portion plate at a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) cooked, chicken stir-fry with vegetables — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung bean), dry (FDC ID 20113); Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (FDC ID 05064); Vegetables, mixed, frozen, cooked (FDC ID 11583); Fish sauce (FDC ID 16424); Chilli and garlic seasoning
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for this Thai restaurant dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked glass noodles (~55% by weight), cooked chicken breast (~25%), mixed vegetables (~15%), and seasoning/sauce (~5%). Per-100g values reflect the cooked mixed dish. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cellophane noodles (mung bean vermicelli) — analogous estimate based on published GI values for mung bean glass noodles (GI ~45, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Thai restaurant dish. GI of 45 is estimated from published values for cooked mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles), which consistently show low GI (~39–53) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. The addition of chicken and vegetables does not substantially raise the GI. GL per serving = (45 × 15.2 × 350 / 100) / 100 = 23.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, which represents over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from glass noodles despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although glass noodles have a low glycemic index (~45), a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 53 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~24, which requires careful portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from stir-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content from Thai seasoning sauces poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions and request preparation with reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Glass Noodles with Chicken and Vegetables (Thai: วุ้นเส้นเผ็ดไก่ผัก, romanized: Wun Sen Phet Kai Phak) is a Thai stir-fried noodle dish featuring glass noodles (tang hoon or mung bean vermicelli), sliced chicken breast, and mixed vegetables, seasoned with chilli, garlic, fish sauce, and oyster sauce. The dish is a staple of Thai casual dining and is commonly served in Thai restaurants in Singapore, including at Sanook Kitchen. Glass noodles are translucent, slippery, and slightly chewy in texture, absorbing the spicy, savoury sauce well. The dish is moderately spicy with umami depth from fish sauce and oyster sauce. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density (~112 kcal per 100 g), lean protein from chicken (~7.8 g per 100 g), and a relatively low glycemic index (~45) due to the mung bean starch structure of glass noodles. However, sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g) due to Thai seasoning sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding lactose. It is not suitable for strict halal diets unless certified, as fish sauce and oyster sauce may contain non-halal processing agents. Clinically, the low GI makes it preferable to rice-based dishes for glycaemic management, but portion control is important due to the moderate glycaemic load of a full serving.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific Thai restaurant dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients (cooked glass noodles, cooked chicken breast, mixed vegetables, Thai seasoning sauces). GI is estimated from published mung bean glass noodle GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as this is a stir-fried dish using vegetable oil with no hydrogenated fat components; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu label '715D Spicy Tang Hoon with Chicken, Veget' to the canonical English name 'Spicy Glass Noodles with Chicken and Vegetables'; the original menu label is preserved in aliases. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce and oyster sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-specific verification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate_g per 100g (15.2) × serving size (350 g) / 100 / 100 = 23.9 g.
